entities.name: grahana
entities.cite: the apprehension of the sound is accepted

axioms.name: avibhagena
axioms.cite: the dhvani is grasped only aviBhagena, in non-separation from the sphota

axioms.name: asamvedya
axioms.cite: the dhvani is asamvedya, never itself an object of awareness at all

axioms.name: svatantra
axioms.cite: it is svatantra, independent — separately audible

claims.name: avibhagena
claims.cite: the apprehension of the sound is accepted as undivided from the form of the sphota

claims.name: asamvedya
claims.cite: the sound is construed as never itself sensed

rejected_readings.label: sound_as_primary
rejected_readings.rendering: Sound is a primary and independently perceivable physical sensation, not necessarily connected to word-meaning or manifestation.
rejected_readings.why: This ignores that all three positions in the verse presuppose sound's manifestive function. The phenomenological dispute concerns how manifestation structures experience, not whether manifestation occurs. Treating sound as a simple sensory datum misses the theoretical frame Bhartrhari establishes.

rejected_readings.label: meaning_without_sound
rejected_readings.rendering: Sphota (meaning) manifests independently of sound; sound is dispensable to the cognition of word-meaning.
rejected_readings.why: All three positions presuppose that sound (dhvani) plays a constitutive role in the grahana (apprehension-and-manifestation process), whether inseparable from, inferred from, or independently of sphota. Eliminating sound's role abandons the phenomenological problem the verse addresses: what is the relationship between sound and word-form in linguistic experience?

rejected_readings.label: asamvedya_as_nonexistence
rejected_readings.rendering: In the asamvedya view, sound is non-existent or not real; meaning manifests entirely without any acoustic substrate or vehicle.
rejected_readings.why: Asamvedya means 'never itself an object of awareness'—an epistemic claim about perception—not an ontological assertion that sound is non-existent. The commentary clarifies: sound is 'a pure vehicle, doing its manifesting work entirely offstage, inferred by theorists but absent from experience.' Sound still functions and exists; it simply escapes perceptual grasp. Confusing the epistemic claim with ontological negation severs the acoustic substrate that all three positions presuppose to play a constitutive role in manifestation.

notes: Verse 1.81 presents three mutually exclusive phenomenological accounts of sound-apprehension: inseparably grasped from sphota, never directly perceived, or independently audible. All presuppose the manifestation-framework. Bhartrhari reports without endorsing any single position; his later accounts of learning (1.82-84) and error (1.85-86) will draw on all three. The contract captures the phenomenological question and the three coherent positions without deciding the matter.
